title: practical english usage - Part 176

A  sentence can have more than one clause, usually joined by a conjunction INTRODUCTION and Clauses Section 20  Conjunctions, Sentences grammar •  Section 20  Conjunctions, Sentences and Clauses general points 229   subordinate clauses: some                           that they be, if I were, etc and; (n)either... (n)or, not only  232    subjunctive: that she go, 228  emphatic coordination: both ...                  subordinate clauses 227   not... or, not... nor, and not   231    tense simplification in but, or                  after prepositions 226   putting things together: and,               230    who, which, what, etc CONTENTS     OF THIS  SECTION for it. ►    231.6 If I had lots of money, I would give some to anybody who  would ask x You’ ll find Coca-Cola wherever you will go. ►    231.1 x x  As you know, that I work very hard. ►    229.5 x   Although she was tired but she went to work. ►    229.5 She was depressed because didn't know what to do. ►    229.3 x x  Neither he smiled nor he spoke. ►    228.3 Both he can borrow the flat and he can use the car. ►    228.1 x Jack isn’ t here neither. ►    227.2 x terms. ►    227.1 x   The  project was not successful in political terms and in economic He doesn’ t smoke nor drink. ►    227.1 x x   He doesn't smoke and drink. ►    226.4 Could I have some butter and bread? ►    226.2 x Do  you   know     what's    wrong     with    these,    and   why?

are dealt with in Sections 22-25.

general points to do with subordination. Particular types of subordinate clause This Section covers co-ordination (of phrases as well as clauses), and some He went up to his room, to find a dog asleep on his bed.

I rushed out of the house, slamming the door behind me.

connected to other clauses with no conjunction.

Participles and infinitives can begin clauses (►   Section 10). These are normally no   conjunction grammar •  226  putting things together: and, but, or not We were uncomfortable and unhappy, (     .. .-u/ia^nforlable^fr-tmhfqqiy;) We were not comfortable or happy.

But or does not join negative adjectives or adverbs. Compare: not He doesn't smoke or drink, (     He doesn't-smoke and drink.) After a negative verb, we  use or, not and.

Would you like tea or coffee? We can meet today or wait until Thursday.

Or also joins grammatically similar expressions.

For but meaning ‘ except’ , »413.

The film started well, but then it went downhill.

We wanted to stay but we simply didn't have time.

Most often, but joins clauses.

They were poor but happy. There were some chairs but no tables.

But can also join grammatically similar expressions.

black and white cup and saucer young and pretty thunder and lightning knife and fork not hands and knees (     knees and hands) not bread and butter (     butter and bread) changed. The  shortest expression often comes first.

Some common expressions with and have a fixed order which  cannot be 2 fixed    expressions:        bread       and     butter,       hands       and     knees For and after try, wait, go, come, etc, ►    99.

For singular and plural verbs after subjects with and, ►     129.5.

For and with adjectives, ►     1H5.   For rules about the use of commas, ►    296.

For details, ►    276.

She's worked in China, (in) Thailand and (in) Brazil.

We need a saw, (a) hammer and (a) screwdriver.

In expressions joined by and, repeated words are often left out My dreams are full of darkness, despair, death.

And is sometimes left out in a very literary or poetic style, but this is unusual.

He started as a cleaner, he worked his way up, and now he runs the company.

We drank, talked and danced.

With more than two expressions, we  generally only put and before the last.

Her English is good and her Japanese is not bad.

You need to go home and have a good rest.

bread and cheese the Prime Minister and his cabinet up to complete clauses.

There are several common ways of doing this.
